_description: Per-question rule tier and rationale for Argentina vs Switzerland QF (2026-07-12). RULE1 applied throughout (Smarkets fetched first). This match's Elo gap (+213.96) is the closest analog this project has had to Spain-Belgium's blowout mismatch (+216.61) since that postmortem was written -- SYMMETRIC_REGIME_COVERAGE was run with real teeth as a result.

questions.text: Will Argentina advance to the semifinals?
questions.rationale: Model (0.7963) and market To-qualify (0.7463) agree within 5pp -- averaged with a small lean toward the model given 3-method convergence. Final: 0.77.

questions.text: Will the match be 0-0 at halftime?
questions.rationale: Model sim (0.3150) and market HT-correct-score 0-0 contract (0.3864) -- 7pp gap, under the mandatory-recheck threshold, averaged. Final: 0.35.

questions.text: Will Switzerland hold a lead at any point?
questions.rationale: No market for this exact path-dependent prop. Monte Carlo sim trusted directly (0.3186).

questions.text: Will the match have 2 or fewer total goals?
questions.rationale: Model (0.5729) and market Under-2.5 (0.5902) agree closely (1.7pp) -- good calibration this match, unlike Norway-England's total-goals lambda issue. Averaged. Final: 0.58.

questions.text: Will a goal be scored after the second hydration break?
questions.rationale: Model sim (0.4593), no exact market. Late-window timing questions are a project-flagged soft spot (KNOCKOUT_STAGE_PRICING_DEEP_DIVE.md) -- nudged toward 0.5 per the standing discipline. Final: 0.47.

Naive 5-game EB-shrunk model (0.8361) checked against Switzerland's demonstrated pattern of collapsing output vs stronger opponents -- regime-adjusted to 0.7594 using only the 2 toughest-opponent games. Still 16.3pp above market (0.5962) after adjustment, so weighted market more heavily in the final blend rather than trusting either model version. Final: 0.62.

questions.text: Will a substitute score or assist?
questions.rationale: Same 60-match corpus base rate built during the Norway-England session (0.55), no match-specific adjustment warranted.

5/5 games scored this tournament (8 goals), an even more extreme streak than Haaland's. Leaned above market (0.4528) given the unprecedented consistency, but RULE6 discipline against near-certain submissions still applies. Final: 0.50.

The largest gap of the session: 0/5 empirical (both goals and assists) plus corroborating low shot volume (5 shots total, 5 games) vs market's 0.50. No historical panel data exists to arbitrate. Matches the exact shape of the Yamal reputation-inflation pattern validated 3 times already this tournament -- market pricing a recognizable attacker on role/reputation rather than current production. Priced well below market but not at the extreme empirical 0%, per RULE6 and small-n caution. Final: 0.32.
Empirical 0.60 (3/5 games) and market (0.5525) agree closely -- averaged. Final: 0.57.
Same mechanism as Q6 -- naive model 0.7137, regime-adjusted (2 toughest opponents only) 0.6080, still 21.1pp above market (0.3968) after adjustment. Weighted market more heavily. Final: 0.45.

Mirror-direction check run: Switzerland's defense held Colombia (the closest-caliber opponent it has faced) to a low SOT-to-shots conversion rate (3 SOT off 15 shots), a real but not overwhelming defensive signal. Model (0.6086 naive, 0.6583 market-calibrated) already agree closely -- no extreme override needed, the moderate defensive discipline appears already reflected in both. Final: 0.63.

No exact market line (Smarkets offers Argentina O/U4.5, not 6.5) -- calibrated an implied lambda via brentq and re-evaluated at 7+. Model (0.1758) and market-calibrated (0.2169) agree closely. Final: 0.20.

Model's team-level own-card rates are low for both sides this tournament, pulling the blend down to 0.2339. Market (exact-match line, 0.4037) likely reflects fixture-specific tension (rivalry framing, 'controversial referee' storyline) the historical-rate model can't see, plus Pinheiro's own admittedly thin sample includes a red card. Weighted toward market. Final: 0.37.

questions.text: Will a penalty be awarded OR a red card shown?
questions.rationale: Model (0.4814) and market-derived OR (0.4205) agree within 6pp -- averaged. Pinheiro's n=2 sample (1 penalty, 1 red across his 2 known matches) is thinner than any referee sample used this project, flagged as a weak signal accordingly. Final: 0.45.
